LAW AND MOTION DEPT. 302 - DEFENDANT LYFT INC.'S MOTION TO TRANSFER VENUE TO CLARK COUNTY, NEVADA IS DENIED. DEFENDANT'S MOTION IS NOT PROPER UNDER CODE OF CIVIL PROCEDURE SECTION 395 OR SECTION 397(C). A CALIFORNIA COURT MAY TRANSFER VENUE TO ANOTHER COUNTY IN CALIFORNIA, BUT NOT TO ANOTHER STATE. (SEE GENERALLY CALIFORNIA PRACTICE GUIDE: CIVIL PROCEDURE BEFORE TRIAL (THE RUTTER GROUP 2020) PAR. 3:450, AT 3-130 ["VENUE RULES DESIGNATE A PARTICULAR COUNTY (OR COUNTIES) WITHIN CALIFORNIA AS THE 'PROPER' PLACE FOR TRIAL OF THE ACTION. SINCE JURISDICTION IS STATEWIDE, THE VENUE RULES SERVE TO NARROW GEOGRAPHICALLY THE PLACE FOR TRIAL."].) THE PROPER MOTION TO SEEK SUCH TRANSFER IS A FORUM NON CONVENIENS MOTION. ***FOR COMPLETE FINDINGS AND RULING SEE MINUTES AND/OR WRITTEN ORDER. PLAINTIFF AGREES TO 30 DAYS FOR RESPONSE TO COMPLAINT.*** WRITTEN ORDER IS SIGNED. JUDGE: ETHAN P. SCHULMAN; CLERK: J. RIOS MERIDA; REPORTER: MARIA TORREANO, CSR NO. 8600, EMAIL: MARIA.TORREANO@GMAIL.COM

Plaintiff Marcella Ann Irwin Freeman?S Notice Of Motion And Motion To Quash Defendant Lyft, Inc.S Deposition Subpoenas For Production Of Business Records; Declaration Of Maximilian Lee With Exhibits Attached Thereto
Off Calendar Oct-04-2021 Granted Per Written Order
MINI MINUTES:

APPEARANCES (VIA ZOOM VIDEOCONFERENCE):
Atty. MAXIMILIAN LEE, Esq. of the Simon Law Group, LLP (Tel: 310-914-5400) for plaintiffs;
No other appearances are made in this matter.

HEARING RE: PLAINTIFF MARCELLA IRWIN FREEMAN'S Motion To Quash Defendant Lyft, Inc.'s Deposition Subpoenas For Production Of Business Records.

Plaintiffs' stipulate to the matter being decided by the Judge Pro Tem. The Judge Pro Tem and counsel for plaintiffs authorize the clerk to sign the formal stipulation form on their behalf.

Matter not contested/argued. The Court adopts its tentative ruling.

Plaintiff Marcella Ann Irwin Freeman's motion is GRANTED. Given the nature of the injuries allegedly suffered by Plaintiff Marcella Ann Irwin Freeman, the scope of the subpoenas is not overly broad or invasive of her right to privacy. However, in combination with the unlimited time frame, the subpoenas are invasive of that right to privacy. While the Defendant may be entitled to discover records reflecting treatments for injuries to similar areas of the body - as reflected in the Plaintiff's response to Form Interrogatory 10.1 - it does not appear that any of the subject subpoenas are directed to any of the providers listed in response to the Form Interrogatory. Accordingly, pursuant to Code of Civil Procedure section 1987.1, the subpoenas shall be modified to be limited in time to five years prior to the incident (i.e., 5 years prior to March 5, 2019).

Order signed.

Judge Pro Tem: Noah Lebowitz; Clerk: Sean Kane; Not Reported. (302/JPT)

Notice Of Motion And Motion To Quash Defendant Lyft, Inc.S Deposition Subpoenas For Production Of Business Records; Declaration Of Maximilian Lee With Exhibits Attached Thereto
Off Calendar Oct-04-2021 Granted Per Written Order
MINI MINUTES:

APPEARANCES (VIA ZOOM VIDEOCONFERENCE):
Atty. MAXIMILIAN LEE, Esq. of the Simon Law Group, LLP (Tel: 310-914-5400) for plaintiffs;
No other appearances are made in this matter.

HEARING RE: PLAINTIFF ERIC FREEMAN's Motion To Quash Defendant Lyft, Inc.'s Deposition Subpoenas For Production Of Business Records.

Plaintiffs' stipulate to the matter being decided by the Judge Pro Tem. The Judge Pro Tem and counsel for plaintiffs authorize the clerk to sign the formal stipulation form on their behalf.

Matter not contested/argued. The Court adopts its tentative ruling.

Plaintiff Eric Thomas Freeman's motion to quash is GRANTED. As presently written, the subpoenas are overly broad in both subject matter and time frame and, as such, violate the Plaintiff's right to privacy. (Cal. Const. Art. I, section 1.) Pursuant to Code of Civil Procedure section 1987.1, the subject subpoenas shall be modified to (a) seek records reflecting treatments related to the Plaintiff's left arm or shoulder; and (b) restricted to a time frame of five years prior to the incident (i.e. 5 years prior to March 5, 2019).

Order signed.

Judge Pro Tem: Noah Lebowitz; Clerk: Sean Kane; Not Reported. (302/JPT)

MOTION TO TRANSFER
Off Calendar Jan-12-2021 Text Ruling
MINI MINUTES:
Appearances via ZOOM Video-conferencing:

Attorney, Maximilian K Lee (Email: Max@JusticeTeam.com) of the law offices of The Simon Law Group LLP (Tel: 310.914.5400) appears as counsel for plaintiff/opposing party

Attorney, Jillian R. Harvey (Email: jharvey@selmanlaw.com) of the law offices of Selman Breitman LLP (Tel: 415.979.0400) appears as counsel for defendant/moving party

DEFENDANT LYFT, INC. MOTION TO TRANSFER VENUE

The cause is not argued or contested, but is discussed briefly on as reflected in the notes of the Certified Shorthand Reporter. Plaintiff's counsel agrees to response to complaint within 30 days.

The Court adopts the tentative ruling. The motion is DENIED.

Defendant's motion is not proper under Code of Civil Procedure section 395 or section 397(c). A California court may transfer venue to another county in California, but not to another state. (See generally California Practice Guide: Civil Procedure Before Trial (The Rutter Group 2020) par. 3:450, at 3-130 ["Venue rules designate a particular county (or counties) within California as the 'proper' place for trial of the action. Since jurisdiction is statewide, the venue rules serve to narrow geographically the place for trial."].) The proper motion to seek such transfer is a forum non conveniens motion.

Venue is proper in this Court. "A corporation or association may be sued in the county where the contract is made or is to be performed, or where the obligation or liability arises, or the breach occurs; or in the county where the principal place of business of such corporation is situated, subject to the power of the court to change the place of trial as in other cases." (Code Civ. Proc. sec. 395.5; see Haas Decl. par. 3 ["Lyft is headquartered in San Francisco, California."].)

Even if Defendant were seeking transfer of venue to another county in California, it has not met its burden under section 397(c). When a party seeks to transfer venue under Code of Civil Procedure section 397(c), "[i]t is not only necessary that the convenience of witnesses be promoted but equally essential that the 'ends of justice' be promoted before the court is justified in granting the motion. The burden of proving both of these conditions is necessarily on the moving party." (Willingham v. Pecora (1941) 44 Cal.App.2d 289, 295.) To meet this burden, "[t]he affidavits in support of the motion for change of venue on this ground must set forth the names of the witnesses, the nature of the testimony expected from each, and the reasons why the attendance of each would be inconvenient." (Peiser v. Mettler (1958) 50 Cal. 2d 594, 607.) Defendant makes no such showing, but merely attaches a traffic collision report, which is not properly authenticated and is inadmissible.

Judge: Ethan P. Schulman; Clerk: J. Rios Merida; Reporter: Maria Torreano, CSR No. 8600, email: maria.torreano@gmail.com
